title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Palm Oil: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of Tropical Commodity Extraction
epigraph: The oil is in everything. The forest was everywhere.
executiveSummary:
The global palm oil industry generates an annual private payoff of $68.0 billion. This private gain is systematically overshadowed by an annual system welfare cost conservatively estimated at $428.3 billion. Applying the System Asset Pricing Model, the system beta (βW) for palm oil is 6.3, with a 90% confidence interval ranging from 4.6 to 8.7. This means that for every dollar of private payoff generated by the palm oil industry, $6.30 in system welfare is destroyed. The industry's system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) is deeply negative, standing at -$360.3 billion per year. This paper proves that the palm oil industry, as currently configured, is a Class III System Welfare Destroyer. This classification signifies that its welfare costs substantially exceed its private payoff, necessitating fundamental restructuring. The probability that the system beta is less than 1.0 (meaning the industry is net welfare-positive) is 0.0000%, as confirmed by a 100,000-draw Monte Carlo simulation. The headline estimate of 6.3 is robust to significant variations in underlying parameters, including a ±40% change in any single welfare channel. The welfare destruction is disaggregated across six distinct channels. The carbon and climate channel, primarily driven by peatland conversion and deforestation, accounts for the largest share of the welfare cost, estimated at $200 billion per year. This represents 48% of the total system welfare cost. The social cost of carbon from tropical peat-to-palm conversion is nine times the economic return from palm oil exports. Biodiversity collapse, transboundary haze and health impacts, water pollution, labor exploitation and land-rights expropriation, and governance and institutional failure contribute the remaining welfare costs. The governance channel, estimated at $12 billion per year, also acts as a multiplier, inflating other welfare costs by approximately 15% due to regulatory capture and information suppression. Achieving Pareto admissibility, where the industry's system-adjusted payoff is non-negative, requires a break-even mitigation rate (μ*) of 0.86. This means the industry must eliminate 86% of its externalized welfare costs. Current interventions, including voluntary certification schemes like the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil and corporate No Deforestation, No Peat, No Exploitation pledges, collectively achieve an actual mitigation rate (μactual) of approximately 0.14. This leaves a significant gap of 72 percentage points, demonstrating that existing mechanisms are profoundly insufficient. The paper identifies the 14/92 peatland asymmetry as the single highest-leverage intervention point. Only 14% of Indonesia's palm oil plantations are located on peatlands, yet they are responsible for 92% of the sector's total greenhouse gas footprint. An absolute peatland moratorium, coupled with hydrological restoration and a mandatory buyback program, would eliminate roughly half of the industry's total system welfare cost at a cost-benefit ratio of 25 to 35 to 1. This intervention, alongside yield intensification on existing mineral-soil plantations, living-wage and International Labour Organization-compliant labor standards, and comprehensive governance reform, forms the necessary four-pronged approach to achieve Pareto admissibility. The Substitution Impossibility Theorem, central to this analysis, proves that no market mechanism satisfying three core axioms—Peat-Carbon Coupling, Supply Chain Deforestation Opacity, and Governance Certification Failure—can reduce the system beta below a floor of 3.4 through private action alone. This welfare floor is structurally embedded in the peatland-cultivation architecture and cannot be closed without exogenous institutional restructuring that violates at least one axiom. The intervention window for implementing these reforms is narrow, approximately 2025 to 2030, before accumulating welfare costs permanently outpace institutional capacity to contain them. The implications are stark. The palm oil industry's economic efficiency is an illusion, subsidized by massive, unpriced destruction of global commons and human welfare. The persistent failure of voluntary and fragmented regulatory efforts over two decades confirms the structural nature of this problem. The System Asset Pricing Model provides the transparent, reproducible metric necessary to make this hidden cost legible, demanding a fundamental re-evaluation of the industry's operating model and the policy frameworks that permit its continued externalization of harm.

keyFindings: The global palm oil industry generates $68.0 billion per year in market value while imposing system welfare costs conservatively estimated at $428.3 billion per year. This yields a system beta (βW) of 6.3, meaning each dollar of private palm oil payoff destroys $6.30 in system welfare. This ratio places palm oil among the most welfare-destructive industries ever calibrated under the System Asset Pricing Model, comparable to per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances manufacturing and exceeding Bitcoin mining. The industry's system-adjusted payoff is deeply negative, at -$360.3 billion per year, indicating a massive net loss to human society.

Peatland conversion alone accounts for 48% of the total welfare destruction attributed to the palm oil industry. The social cost of carbon from tropical peat-to-palm conversion is nine times the economic return from palm oil exports, as demonstrated by Kauffman et al. (2025). This highlights peatlands as a critical vulnerability and a disproportionate driver of climate-related welfare costs. The continuous oxidation of drained peatlands releases approximately 100 tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per hectare per year, a process that persists for decades after initial conversion.

Indonesia's 2015 peat fires released approximately 1 gigatonne of carbon dioxide equivalent in a single season. This catastrophic event inflicted $16 billion in direct economic damage, according to the World Bank, and between $37 billion and $185 billion in global climate welfare loss, depending on the social cost of carbon applied. The daily emissions during the peak fire period in September and October 2015 surpassed the daily carbon emissions of the entire European Union economy for several weeks. These fires also caused 110,000 premature deaths annually from transboundary haze, demonstrating the immediate and severe health impacts.

Achieving Pareto admissibility for the palm oil industry requires a break-even mitigation rate (μ*) of 0.86, meaning 86% of externalized harm must be eliminated. However, existing voluntary certification schemes, such as the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil, cover only 19% of global production and demonstrably fail to reduce peatland clearing or fire incidence within certified concessions. Corporate No Deforestation, No Peat, No Exploitation pledges, covering 74% to 80% of refining capacity, also exhibit significant leakage. The industry's actual achieved mitigation rate (μactual) is approximately 0.14, falling six-fold short of the required threshold.

A mere 1.8% increase in consumer end-product prices, totaling $6 billion per year across the downstream Fast-Moving Consumer Goods sector, would be sufficient to fund full living-wage compliance and smallholder replanting across the entire palm oil supply chain. The downstream Fast-Moving Consumer Goods sector captures an estimated $20 billion per year in palm-oil-derived profits. The refusal of this sector to internalize this cost for two decades demonstrates that labor exploitation and environmental destruction are not accidental market failures but deliberate, structural features optimized for wealth extraction.

Palm oil is classified as Class III — System Welfare Destroyer in the cross-domain System Asset Pricing Model registry. This classification places it between Monoculture Agriculture (βW = 8.6) and Bitcoin mining (βW = 5.0). Class III industries are characterized by welfare costs substantially exceeding private payoffs, necessitating fundamental restructuring. This positioning allows for direct welfare-cost comparisons with other industries, confirming palm oil's distinct and severe position on the system welfare spectrum.

This paper provides the first System Asset Pricing Model calibration of a tropical agricultural commodity. Previous System Asset Pricing Model applications focused on industrial chemicals like per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, energy systems such as Bitcoin and nuclear energy, and technology like Frontier Artificial Intelligence. The palm oil calibration extends the framework to the intersection of agriculture, land-use change, and global commodity markets, a domain where 16.2 million people depend directly on the extractive activity and where the largest absolute welfare costs reside.

The system beta (βW) is disaggregated into six channel-specific betas, spanning biophysical, health, labor, and governance costs. Peatland carbon (βW,1 = 2.98) and biodiversity collapse (βW,2 = 0.75) are identified as the dominant drivers of welfare destruction. Governance failure (βW,6 = 0.18) operates as a multiplier on all other channels, inflating total welfare costs by approximately 15% due to regulatory capture, Strategic Lawsuits Against Public Participation litigation, and certification-scheme co-optation. This decomposition provides granular insight into the mechanisms of welfare destruction.

Empirical evidence confirms that voluntary certification schemes, specifically the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil, demonstrably fail to meet the Pareto-admissibility threshold. Quasi-experimental studies, such as Carlson et al. (2018), found that Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil certification was associated with only a 33% reduction in deforestation, primarily in areas with minimal remaining forest, and showed no significant effect on fire incidence or peatland clearing. With an actual mitigation rate of approximately 0.14, Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil and other voluntary efforts fall six-fold short of the 0.86 break-even rate required.

Peatland conversion is identified as the single highest-leverage intervention point. Only 14% of Indonesia's palm oil plantations are located on peatlands, yet these plantations generate 92% of the sector's greenhouse gas footprint. This 14/92 asymmetry creates a narrow and highly effective policy target: an absolute peatland moratorium combined with hydrological restoration yields the steepest Pareto System Frontier improvement per dollar of foregone private return. Eliminating peatland production would reduce private payoff by only 15% while reducing system welfare costs by approximately 48%.

The cumulative Tropical Extraction Deficit for palm oil from 1990 to 2025 is estimated at approximately $6 trillion to $10 trillion in uncompensated welfare destruction. This deficit, which represents the total welfare bill society has absorbed to subsidize private returns, exceeds Indonesia's entire gross domestic product by a factor of four to seven. This intergenerational carbon debt includes committed future emissions from already-drained peatlands, totaling approximately 6.75 gigatonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent, valued at $1.25 trillion at the current social cost of carbon.

The System Asset Pricing Model framework enables a cross-domain beta ranking, positioning palm oil within a broader registry of industries. This allows for direct welfare-cost comparisons with industries such as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (βW = 35.2), Bitcoin (βW = 5.0), and nuclear energy (βW = 0.7). Palm oil's position at βW = 6.3 confirms that tropical commodity extraction occupies a distinct and distinctly severe position on the system welfare spectrum, requiring a unique blend of biophysical and institutional reforms.

theorem.formal:
The Substitution Impossibility Theorem states that no market mechanism satisfying three specific axioms—Peat-Carbon Coupling, Supply Chain Deforestation Opacity, and Governance Certification Failure—can reduce the system beta (βW) for the palm oil industry below a floor of 3.4 through private action alone. This theorem asserts that the welfare floor is structurally embedded in the peatland-cultivation architecture and cannot be closed without exogenous institutional restructuring that violates at least one of these axioms. The theorem implies that the welfare cost is structurally irreducible under the domain's axiom set, meaning that market-based instruments or voluntary commitments are insufficient to close the welfare gap. The three axioms are:

Axiom A1 (Peat-Carbon Coupling): Palm oil cultivation on tropical peatlands releases carbon accumulated over millennia at rates that cannot be offset by any agricultural productivity gain. Draining a peatland for oil palm cultivation immediately releases 40 to 100 tonnes of carbon dioxide per hectare in the first year and continues releasing 12 to 50 tonnes of carbon dioxide per year for decades thereafter. These emissions are a direct physical consequence of the drainage that enables cultivation. The drainage that generates private payoff by enabling high-yield oil palm cultivation simultaneously generates system welfare destruction through irreversible peat oxidation that no operational efficiency, certification scheme, or carbon offset can reverse. Eliminating the drainage eliminates the yield advantage, collapsing the economic case for palm oil over alternatives.

Axiom A2 (Supply Chain Deforestation Opacity): Palm oil enters global supply chains through hundreds of mills, thousands of smallholder suppliers, and commodity trading intermediaries that obscure the connection between a consumer product and a specific forest clearance event. Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil traceability covers only 30% of palm oil production to the mill level and 8% to the plantation level. Concession maps in Indonesia and Malaysia are not publicly available at the resolution required to verify No Deforestation, No Peat, No Exploitation commitments at the mill catchment level. The deforestation welfare cost is geographically and causally invisible at the supply chain transaction level.

Axiom A3 (Governance Certification Failure): The Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil, the primary voluntary certification system, has been structurally captured by industry interests. This capture preserves the certification mechanism while preventing effective enforcement of its purported standards. Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil-certified palm oil continues to be associated with deforestation, its complaints procedures are slow and rarely result in sanctions, and the certification premium is insufficient to compensate for the land value of forest retention. The European Union Deforestation Regulation, a key regulatory instrument, was itself delayed by industry lobbying, demonstrating the susceptibility of even robust regulations to capture.

The theorem concludes that the welfare floor of βW ≥ 3.4 is the minimum system beta achievable under these conditions, representing a welfare cost embedded in the peat-carbon-governance architecture that cannot be reduced through market-based instruments or voluntary certification within the current institutional framework.
theorem.plain:
Imagine a factory that makes a product everyone wants, say, a super-efficient car. This car is cheap to make and runs on a special fuel. But there's a hidden catch: the fuel comes from a unique type of swamp, and extracting it releases a toxic gas that slowly poisons the air for everyone, everywhere. The factory owners know this, but it's cheaper to keep extracting the fuel than to find a clean alternative or clean up the mess. That's the core idea behind the Substitution Impossibility Theorem for palm oil.

Here's the breakdown
theorem.simplified:
n
First, imagine the 'toxic gas' is the carbon dioxide released when palm oil plantations drain tropical peatlands. These peatlands are like giant sponges
theorem.Peat-Carbon Coupling:
you can't grow palm oil on these lands without releasing this carbon. It's a physical law. It's like saying you can't burn wood without making smoke. The palm oil is incredibly efficient on this land, but that efficiency comes at the cost of massive, unavoidable carbon emissions.

Second, imagine the 'toxic gas' is invisible to the car buyers. They just see the cheap car. This is Axiom 2
theorem.Supply Chain Deforestation Opacity:
the journey of palm oil from a remote plantation to your supermarket shelf is so long and complicated that it's impossible for you, the consumer, or even the big companies buying the oil, to know exactly where it came from or if it caused deforestation. It's like the car manufacturer hiding where they get their fuel, making it impossible for you to choose a 'clean' car, even if you wanted to. The destruction is hidden by the complexity of the global supply chain.

Third, imagine there's a 'green label' for cars, saying they're 'sustainable.' But the company giving out the labels is funded by the car manufacturers, and they're very lenient. They certify cars that were already built
theorem.Governance Certification Failure:
the main 'sustainable palm oil' label, the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil, has been co-opted by the industry. It doesn't actually stop deforestation or peatland destruction effectively. It's like the green label being a marketing tool, not a real guarantee of environmental protection.

So, the theorem says: as long as these three things are true – you can't grow palm oil on peatlands without carbon emissions (Axiom 1), you can't track where the palm oil comes from (Axiom 2), and the 'green' labels don't actually enforce real change (Axiom 3) – then the palm oil industry will always destroy far more welfare than it creates. It's an 'impossibility' because, under these conditions, no amount of private effort or market choice can make the industry genuinely welfare-positive. The problem isn't just bad actors; it's the fundamental structure of the system.

axioms.name: Peat-Carbon Coupling
axioms.statement: Palm oil cultivation on tropical peatlands releases carbon accumulated over millennia at rates that cannot be offset by any agricultural productivity gain. Draining a peatland for oil palm cultivation immediately releases 40–100 tonnes of carbon dioxide per hectare in the first year and continues releasing 12–50 tonnes of carbon dioxide per year for decades thereafter—emissions that are a direct physical consequence of the drainage that makes cultivation possible.
axioms.evidence: Hooijer et al. (2010, 2012) established that drained peatlands emit approximately 100 tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per hectare per year through microbial decomposition. Kauffman et al. (2025) quantified initial stock losses from primary peat swamp forest conversion at 640 tonnes of carbon dioxide per hectare. The paper states that peat drainage is not a side effect but the enabling condition for palm oil's yield advantage, and eliminating drainage eliminates this advantage. This physical reality is robustly documented by peat biogeochemistry research.

axioms.name: Supply Chain Deforestation Opacity
axioms.statement: Palm oil enters global supply chains through hundreds of mills, thousands of smallholder suppliers, and commodity trading intermediaries that obscure the connection between a consumer product and a specific forest clearance event. Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil traceability covers only 30% of palm oil production to mill level and 8% to plantation level (WWF 2023).
axioms.evidence: WWF's 2023 data confirms the limited traceability depth of the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil. Rainforest Action Network's 2024 investigation traced illegal palm oil from protected areas to major Fast-Moving Consumer Goods companies, demonstrating the practical opacity. The paper notes that concession maps in Indonesia and Malaysia are not publicly available at the resolution required for plot-level verification, making it impossible for manufacturers to verify No Deforestation, No Peat, No Exploitation commitments across indirect smallholder suppliers.

axioms.name: Governance Certification Failure
axioms.statement: The Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil—the primary voluntary certification system—has been structurally captured by industry interests that preserve the certification mechanism while preventing enforcement of the standards it purports to represent.
axioms.evidence: Carlson et al. (2018) found that Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil certification did not significantly reduce deforestation or fire incidence. Cazzolla Gatti et al. (2020) showed that up to 75% of Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil concessions were fully forested 30 years ago, yet received certification due to a lenient cutoff date. The Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil's grievance mechanism took 2.5 years to suspend Indofood and nearly a decade for Dayak Hibun complaints. The European Union Deforestation Regulation's delay by industry lobbying in 2024 further evidences the susceptibility of regulatory instruments to capture.

theorem.proofSketch:
The Substitution Impossibility Theorem for palm oil establishes a structural welfare floor, meaning that the industry's system beta (βW) cannot be reduced below a certain threshold (3.4) through market-based or voluntary actions alone. The proof proceeds by demonstrating how the three axioms—Peat-Carbon Coupling, Supply Chain Deforestation Opacity, and Governance Certification Failure—jointly prevent the internalization of welfare costs, thereby maintaining a high βW.

Step 1: Axiom A1 (Peat-Carbon Coupling) establishes an irreducible biophysical welfare cost. Palm oil cultivation on tropical peatlands inherently involves drainage, which in turn causes continuous carbon dioxide emissions. This is a physical necessity for the crop to grow with its characteristic high yields. The paper quantifies this cost: 100 tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per hectare per year from drained peatlands, valued at $185 per tonne of carbon dioxide. This means that for every hectare of peatland palm oil, a significant and unavoidable welfare cost is generated. This cost is not a 'side effect' that can be managed away; it is a direct consequence of the production method. Therefore, any private payoff (Π) generated from peatland palm oil will always be accompanied by a substantial welfare displacement (δ1) that cannot be eliminated while maintaining the production. This sets a minimum, non-zero welfare cost for a significant portion of the industry.

Step 2: Axiom A2 (Supply Chain Deforestation Opacity) ensures that this biophysical welfare cost, along with others like biodiversity loss from deforestation, remains externalized and invisible to market mechanisms. Because the supply chain is fragmented and lacks plot-level traceability, consumers and even downstream Fast-Moving Consumer Goods companies cannot reliably identify whether their palm oil originates from recently deforested or peatland-converted areas. This opacity prevents market signals (e.g., consumer preference for 'sustainable' products) from effectively translating into higher prices for genuinely sustainable palm oil or lower demand for destructive palm oil. Without this transparency, the market cannot price in the welfare costs, and thus, these costs remain external to the private payoff calculation. This axiom prevents the 'discovery' of the true welfare cost within bilateral transactions, making Pigouvian taxation or Coasean bargaining impossible at scale.

Step 3: Axiom A3 (Governance Certification Failure) confirms that even non-market, voluntary governance mechanisms are insufficient to overcome the opacity and the biophysical constraints. The Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil, despite its widespread adoption, fails to demonstrably reduce deforestation or peatland conversion. Its structural weaknesses—such as lenient cutoff dates, slow grievance mechanisms, and industry capture—mean it does not effectively enforce standards that would break the Peat-Carbon Coupling or demand supply chain transparency. This axiom demonstrates that the institutional architecture designed to mitigate these externalities is itself compromised, allowing the welfare costs to persist and compound. It prevents the 'exogenous restructuring' that the theorem states is necessary.

Jointly, these three axioms create a situation where the welfare costs are physically unavoidable (A1), market-invisible (A2), and institutionally unaddressed (A3). The theorem's conclusion, that βW cannot fall below 3.4 through private action alone, is derived from the aggregated minimum welfare costs that persist even under optimistic interpretations of current market and governance conditions. The Monte Carlo simulation, which shows that 0.026% of draws fall below this floor, statistically confirms its structural character. The proof implies that without a fundamental change to the physical production location (moving off peatlands), the transparency of the supply chain, and the integrity of governance mechanisms, the palm oil industry will continue to generate welfare destruction far exceeding its private benefits, regardless of voluntary efforts or market preferences.

welfareAnalysis.pi: $68.0 billion per year, representing the aggregate market value of crude palm oil, palm kernel oil, and derivatives, net of private production costs.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: $428.3 billion per year, representing the aggregate system welfare destruction across six channels, including the governance loading factor.

channels.name: Carbon and Climate
channels.description: This channel captures the global climate welfare cost arising from peatland drainage, deforestation, and fires associated with palm oil cultivation. Drained peatlands continuously emit approximately 100 tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent per hectare per year. Conversion of primary peat swamp forest releases an estimated 640 tonnes of carbon dioxide per hectare as an upfront stock loss. Fire events, such as Indonesia's 2015 fires, dramatically amplify these emissions, releasing about 1 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent in a single season. The welfare cost is calculated by applying the social cost of carbon, estimated at $185 per tonne of carbon dioxide (Rennert et al. 2022), to the annualized emission profile, including a weighted probability of severe fire years. This channel is the largest contributor to total welfare cost, representing 48% of the total, and its channel-specific beta is 2.98.

channels.name: Biodiversity Collapse
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare cost from habitat destruction, species loss, and trophic collapse resulting from the conversion of biodiverse tropical forests to monoculture oil palm plantations. Fitzherbert et al. (2008) demonstrated that only 15% of primary-forest species persist in oil palm plantations. Palm oil expansion is a primary threat to critically endangered species like orangutans, Sumatran tigers, and Sumatran rhinoceros. The dollar estimate is derived from two independent approaches: bottom-up shadow pricing of biodiversity conservation (Dalheimer et al. 2024) and top-down natural capital accounting (TEEBAgriFood). The estimate includes an upward adjustment for the existence and bequest values of charismatic megafauna, which are difficult to monetize precisely. This channel contributes 12% of the total welfare cost, with a channel-specific beta of 0.75.

channels.name: Transboundary Haze and Health
channels.description: This channel measures the welfare cost from toxic particulate matter 2.5 haze generated by peat fires, which blankets Southeast Asia annually. Chronic exposure to this haze leads to an estimated 110,000 premature deaths per year across the region (Koplitz et al. 2016). Beyond mortality, the economic costs include emergency healthcare, lost labor productivity, school closures, flight cancellations, and tourism collapse. The 2015 haze crisis alone inflicted $16 billion in direct economic costs to Indonesia, according to the World Bank. The $16 billion annual estimate is a weighted average across fire-year severity, using a conservative direct-economic-cost methodology rather than higher Value of Statistical Life-based approaches. This channel accounts for 4% of the total welfare cost, with a channel-specific beta of 0.24.

channels.name: Water Pollution
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare cost from the discharge of Palm Oil Mill Effluent and agrochemical runoff into waterways. Palm Oil Mill Effluent is highly polluting, with a Biochemical Oxygen Demand 100 times greater than untreated domestic sewage, leading to eutrophication, marine dead zones, and contamination of drinking water. Mills often use cheap open ponding systems, paying nominal fines that are far below the true marginal abatement cost of zero-liquid-discharge technology. The dollar estimate is derived from scaling municipal treatment costs, marginal abatement costs for zero-liquid-discharge compliance across approximately 2,000 active mills, and local fishery losses. This channel is the smallest in absolute magnitude, contributing less than 1% of the total welfare cost, with a channel-specific beta of 0.05, but its persistence signals institutional failure.
channels.attributionRate: Data not available in source paper

channels.name: Labor Exploitation and Land-Rights Expropriation
channels.description: This channel captures the human welfare cost from forced labor, child labor, land dispossession, and wage suppression within the palm oil industry. The United States Customs and Border Protection found all 11 International Labour Organization forced labor indicators present in operations of major palm oil companies. Children as young as eight perform hazardous work, and migrant workers face debt bondage. Customary land rights are systematically unrecognized, leading to expropriation. Chain Reaction Research quantified the annual labor and environmental exploitation gap at $6 billion, representing the minimum cost to implement living wages and No Deforestation, No Peat, No Exploitation compliance. The $8 billion central estimate includes an additional $2 billion for health, cultural, and food-security costs. This channel contributes 2% of the total welfare cost, with a channel-specific beta of 0.12.
channels.attributionRate: Data not available in source paper

channels.name: Governance and Institutional Failure
channels.description: This channel quantifies the direct welfare cost of regulatory capture, Strategic Lawsuits Against Public Participation litigation, and certification-scheme co-optation. The industry spends an estimated $40 million to $75 million per year on political expenditure to maintain a regulatory environment that permits externalization of other welfare costs. This expenditure generates welfare costs through information suppression, intimidation of civil society, and the distortion of state institutions from welfare-maximizers into industry promoters. This channel also operates as a multiplier, inflating other welfare costs by approximately 15% (a loading factor of 1.15) due to institutional dysfunction. The $12 billion central estimate represents the standalone costs of this failure. This channel contributes 3% of the total welfare cost, with a channel-specific beta of 0.18.
channels.attributionRate: Data not available in source paper

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
A Monte Carlo simulation was conducted with 100,000 draws to test the robustness of the headline system beta estimate. Each of the six welfare channels was modeled using specific probability distributions reflecting the range of credible estimates from the literature. The carbon and climate channel (δ1), biodiversity collapse channel (δ2), transboundary haze and health channel (δ3), labor exploitation and land-rights expropriation channel (δ5), and governance and institutional failure channel (δ6) were modeled using triangular distributions. The water pollution channel (δ4) and the governance loading factor (λ6) were modeled using uniform distributions, reflecting higher uncertainty or a broader plausible range. The private payoff (Π) was fixed at $68.0 billion, as industry revenue is well-documented and not a significant source of uncertainty. The simulation results confirmed the robustness of the headline estimate. The mean system beta was 6.18, and the median was 6.12, closely aligning with the central estimate of 6.3 derived from point estimates. The 90% confidence interval for the system beta was [4.6, 8.7], indicating that the true value is highly likely to fall within this range. A critical finding from the Monte Carlo simulation is that the probability of the system beta being less than 3.0 (the Class III threshold) is 0.003%, and the probability of it being less than 1.0 (net welfare-positive) is 0.0000%. This demonstrates that even under the most favorable assumptions for all parameters, the palm oil industry remains firmly classified as a System Welfare Destroyer. Sensitivity analysis, including one-at-a-time variations and classification invariance tests, further underscored the robustness. Even if all welfare channels were simultaneously set to their minimum plausible values, the system beta remained at 3.46, still within Class III. If the governance loading factor was entirely removed, the system beta was 3.27, still Class III. Only an implausible scenario involving simultaneous minimum values for all six channels, no governance loading, and a doubling of the industry's documented revenue would shift the classification to Class II (βW ≈ 1.63). This multi-extreme scenario is not credible. The Monte Carlo simulation confirmed that the Class III designation survives all plausible single and pairwise parameter variations, establishing a structural impossibility floor for the system beta. The primary driver of uncertainty in the system beta is the carbon and climate channel, due to the wide range of social cost of carbon estimates and emission volume uncertainties, but even this variability does not alter the fundamental classification.

caseStudies.title: Indonesia's 2015 Peat Fires and Transboundary Haze Crisis
caseStudies.content:
The 2015 Indonesian peat fires represent a catastrophic illustration of the palm oil industry's system welfare destruction, particularly through the carbon and climate, and transboundary haze and health channels. Triggered by an El Niño drought interacting with widespread drainage of peatlands for oil palm cultivation, these fires burned approximately 2.6 million hectares, including 600,000 hectares of peatland, between July and October 2015. The scale of the environmental damage was immense. The fires released an estimated 0.8 to 1.1 gigatonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent in a single three-month period, a volume comparable to Japan's entire annual emissions. During the peak fire period in September and October 2015, daily Indonesian emissions exceeded the daily emissions of the entire European Union economy for 40 consecutive days. This event alone contributed significantly to the carbon and climate channel's annual welfare cost, demonstrating the episodic but devastating spikes in system welfare destruction.

The human cost was equally severe. The fires generated toxic particulate matter 2.5 haze that blanketed Southeast Asia, causing an estimated 110,000 premature deaths across Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, and southern Thailand (Koplitz et al. 2016). The World Bank conservatively estimated the direct economic damage to Indonesia alone at $16 billion, equivalent to 1.8% of the country's Gross Domestic Product, and more than double the damage caused by the 2004 Boxing Day tsunami. This figure captures healthcare expenditure, lost labor productivity, school closures, flight cancellations, and tourism collapse. The global climate welfare loss from these fires ranged from $37 billion to $185 billion, depending on the social cost of carbon applied. The 2015 crisis starkly revealed the immediate and far-reaching consequences of peatland conversion for palm oil, demonstrating how a localized land-use practice can generate global climate impacts and regional public health emergencies, with costs vastly exceeding any private economic benefit.

caseStudies.title: United States Customs and Border Protection Withhold Release Orders Against Sime Darby and FGV Holdings
caseStudies.content:
The United States Customs and Border Protection's issuance of Withhold Release Orders against Sime Darby Plantation Berhad in December 2020 and FGV Holdings Berhad in September 2020 provides a concrete example of how targeted trade enforcement can address the labor exploitation and land-rights expropriation channel. These orders, issued under 19 United States Code §1307, prohibited the importation of palm oil and palm oil products produced by forced labor into the United States. The investigations by United States Customs and Border Protection found evidence of all 11 International Labour Organization indicators of forced labor in the operations of both companies, including debt bondage, retention of identity documents, abusive working and living conditions, and physical and sexual violence.

The impact of these Withhold Release Orders was significant. Sime Darby, one of the world's largest palm oil producers, undertook a comprehensive structural overhaul of its labor practices. This included revising recruitment protocols, reimbursing migrant workers for illicit recruitment fees, and establishing independent human rights governance mechanisms. Following these extensive remediation efforts, the Withhold Release Order against Sime Darby was lifted in February 2023. FGV Holdings also underwent a multi-year structural overhaul, leading to the lifting of its ban in January 2026. Across Malaysia's palm oil and glove sectors, United States Customs and Border Protection enforcement actions resulted in the repayment of over $85 million in withheld wages and recruitment fees to workers trapped in debt bondage. While the United States Customs and Border Protection bans achieved profound structural reductions in forced labor within the specific targeted entities, their overall impact on the industry's system beta is limited. The intervention addresses only a small fraction of the labor channel, which itself constitutes only 2% of the total system welfare cost. Furthermore, the entity-specific nature of the bans means that companies unable to meet United States labor standards can redirect their products to jurisdictions without similar import restrictions, highlighting the challenge of market bifurcation and leakage in addressing systemic welfare destruction.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework:
The current policy and regulatory framework governing the palm oil industry is a fragmented patchwork of voluntary certifications, national regulations, and nascent international trade measures. This multi-layered approach attempts to address the industry's externalities but consistently falls short of achieving Pareto admissibility. Key components include: 1. The Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil: Established in 2004, the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil is the primary voluntary multi-stakeholder certification scheme. It sets Principles and Criteria for sustainable palm oil production, covering environmental protection, social responsibility, and economic viability. As of 2024, it certifies approximately 19% of global production. Its jurisdiction is transnational, relying on member compliance and third-party audits. 2. Corporate No Deforestation, No Peat, No Exploitation Commitments: Major palm oil traders and Fast-Moving Consumer Goods companies have adopted No Deforestation, No Peat, No Exploitation policies, covering an estimated 74% to 80% of Indonesian and Malaysian refining capacity. These are voluntary corporate policies, often driven by consumer and non-governmental organization pressure, aiming to remove deforestation, peatland destruction, and labor exploitation from their supply chains. Their jurisdiction is corporate, extending through supply chain procurement. 3. Indonesian Moratorium on New Concessions: Indonesia implemented a moratorium on new licenses for logging and palm oil concessions in primary forests and peatlands in 2011, made permanent in 2019. This is a national regulatory measure, aiming to curb land-use change. The Ministry of Environment and Forestry and the Peatland Restoration Agency (now Peatland and Mangrove Restoration Agency) have jurisdiction. 4. Malaysian Sustainable Palm Oil: Malaysia has a mandatory national certification scheme, Malaysian Sustainable Palm Oil, covering over 95% of its production. It sets national standards for sustainability, including environmental management and social responsibility. The Malaysian Palm Oil Board has jurisdiction. 5. European Union Renewable Energy Directive II and European Union Deforestation Regulation: The European Union Renewable Energy Directive II classified palm oil as a high indirect land-use change risk feedstock, mandating its phase-out from European Union renewable energy targets by 2030. The European Union Deforestation Regulation, effective 2025-2026, prohibits the import of palm oil and derived products unless they are deforestation-free and legally produced, requiring plot-level geolocation traceability. The European Commission and member state competent authorities have jurisdiction. 6. United States Customs and Border Protection Withhold Release Orders: Under 19 United States Code §1307, the United States Customs and Border Protection can prohibit the importation of goods produced by forced labor. It has issued Withhold Release Orders against major palm oil producers like Sime Darby and FGV Holdings. This is a national trade enforcement tool, with jurisdiction over imports into the United States.
